moveAnnotations.annotation: We start with an unusual move that begins the Hippopotamus Attack—a solid, patient setup where we build a fortress-like pawn structure before launching any kingside attack. The move a3 prepares to advance the b-pawn and prevents Black from using b4 to harass our pieces later. This isn't a direct assault; instead, we're establishing control over our half of the board and waiting for Black to commit their pieces so we can exploit any weaknesses.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: a3 prepares b4, building a fortress pawn structure before launching a kingside attack.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black seizes space in the center immediately, which is natural, but this allows us to execute our Hippopotamus plan with extra precision. By playing e5, Black has committed their central pawn, and we can now build around it with our methodical pawn chain a3–b3–c3–d3–e3–f3–g3.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black's e5 commits the center, enabling White's Hippopotamus pawn chain a3–b3–c3–d3–e3–f3–g3.

moveAnnotations.annotation: We continue our signature setup, advancing the b-pawn and preparing to fianchetto our light-squared bishop on b2, where it will control the long diagonal and support our entire formation. Notice we're playing every move with the same idea: creating a rock-solid base before our pieces even develop. This move also keeps our position flexible and prevents Black from executing an aggressive plan.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Advancing the b-pawn prepares the fianchetto on b2, controlling the long diagonal and keeping the position flexible.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black pushes in the center again, occupying d5 with force. This is actually what we want—Black is spending moves on central play while we're methodically constructing our defensive wall. Their pawns on d5 and e5 are advanced but not yet supported by pieces, which may become a target later.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black's advanced d5 and e5 pawns are unsupported pieces, potential targets for White's developing wall.

moveAnnotations.annotation: The third pawn in our chain advances, supporting the d4 square and preparing to eventually bring in our queen and bishop along the long diagonal. The move c3 is crucial because it gives us flexibility to play d3 and e3 without leaving any squares hanging. We're essentially saying to Black: 'Play aggressively if you like, but we have time to build the perfect setup.'
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: c3 reinforces the d4 pawn chain, preparing d3 and e3 to secure the center and open the long diagonal for queen and bishop.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black develops their kingside knight toward the center, a logical move that pressures e4 and d4 indirectly while preparing to castle kingside. This is solid play from Black, but they're following a conventional development plan while we maintain our unique, positional approach.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black develops the knight to f6, pressuring e4 and d4 while preparing kingside castling.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Now we move the d-pawn forward, completing the skeleton of our setup and controlling key central squares like e4 and c4. The d3 pawn also prepares to support our kingside when we eventually fianchetto our king's pawn on g2. Each pawn move has been carefully ordered to maintain maximum flexibility.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: d3 controls e4 and c4, supporting a kingside fianchetto on g2.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black develops the queen's knight, placing it actively on c6 where it eyes d4 and e4 as potential outposts. Their development is clean and purposeful, but we still maintain our patient, territorial approach. Notice that Black is spending moves on piece development while we're completing our pawn structure.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black's knight on c6 eyes d4 and e4, but White's pawn structure outpaces Black's piece development.

moveAnnotations.annotation: The fourth pawn of our chain slides forward, controlling d4 and f4 while making room for our kingside pawn on f2 or our queen on e2. The e3 pawn is the final central component of our fortress; from here, our position becomes nearly impenetrable. This move also ensures that after we fianchetto both bishops, our king will have a safe home and our minor pieces will dominate.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: The e3 pawn locks the center, controls d4 and f4, and prepares a fianchetto fortress with both bishops dominating.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black places their light-squared bishop actively on d6, aiming toward our kingside and eyeing h2. This is one of Black's more forcing moves, showing they want to create threats rather than simply develop mechanically. However, our solid pawn chain remains unimpressed by this activity.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black's bishop on d6 aims at h2, but White's solid pawn chain neutralizes the threat.

moveAnnotations.annotation: We advance the f-pawn to prepare our kingside fianchetto, and importantly, this move prevents Black from playing ...e4, which would be uncomfortable for us. The f3 pawn also supports the e4 square and creates a solid base for our king's safety. Every single pawn on our first four ranks now supports the next rank forward—we're creating an almost impregnable setup.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: f3 prevents ...e4, supports e4, and builds a solid pawn chain from the first four ranks.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black castles kingside, moving their king to safety and connecting their rooks—a prudent decision that completes their development. Their position is solid, but we've now established our complete pawn structure and can begin our piece placement with a huge positional advantage.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black castles kingside, but White's huge positional advantage now begins piece placement.

moveAnnotations.annotation: The final pawn in our famous chain advances, preparing the ultimate fianchetto of our king's bishop on g2, where it will command the long diagonal and provide devastating support for our entire position. This completes the Hippopotamus setup: a wall of pawns (a3–b3–c3–d3–e3–f3–g3) that controls the center and prepares to channel our pieces into perfectly harmonious squares. Black now faces a fortress they must methodically break down while we prepare to unleash our bishops on the diagonals.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: The Hippopotamus setup's final pawn advances to g3, fianchettoing the king's bishop on g2 to command the long diagonal.
